description: Equation (1) defines the critical grain size \( D^{*} \) below which the emission of full lattice dislocations via a Frank-Read-type bowing mechanism is inhibited. In this expression, \( \mu \) denotes the shear modulus, \( b \) is the magnitude of the Burgers vector for a full dislocation, \( \sigma_{y}(D^{*}) \) represents the macroscopic yield strength as a function of the grain size itself, and \( m \) is the Taylor factor, where \( 1/m \) corresponds to the Schmid factor on the most favorably oriented slip system. The relationship captures the condition where the stress required to operate a dislocation source of length \( D^{*} \) equals the resolved shear stress needed for yielding; it thus establishes a threshold grain size governing the transition in deformation mechanism from the activity of full dislocations to the preferential nucleation of partial dislocations.

The equation $(1/m = 1/2)$ defines a specific condition for the Schmid factor, where $m$ denotes the orientation factor relating the applied stress to the resolved shear stress on a slip system. In this context, $1/m$ represents the Schmid factor itself. The equality $1/m = 1/2$ corresponds to the maximum theoretically possible value of the Schmid factor, which identifies the most favorably oriented slip system for dislocation glide under an applied load. This condition signifies that dislocation bowing and propagation are most active on this primary system, while other, less favorably oriented systems experience inhibited slip. The specific value of $1/2$ is used as a benchmark to calculate critical microstructural dimensions, such as the grain size $D^{*(1/2)}$, below which only partial dislocation emission is possible.

The equation \(1 / m = 1 / 3\) defines the average Schmid factor for a polycrystalline material, where \(m\) denotes the Taylor factor. The Schmid factor, \(1/m\), represents the ratio of resolved shear stress on a slip system to the applied tensile stress. In this context, the specific value of \(1/3\) is adopted as a polycrystalline average to approximate the critical condition for the transition in deformation mechanisms. This average value is used to calculate critical grain sizes, \(D^{*(1/3)}\), marking the onset where full dislocation activity gives way to deformation dominated by partial dislocations and associated defects.

The equation \( { x } = 2 \) defines the variable \( x \), which in this context denotes the purity level of a copper sample. Here, \( x \) represents the number of nines in the weight percentage purity, such that a sample labeled "xN" corresponds to a purity of \( 10^{-x} \) weight percent impurities. Specifically, \( x = 2 \) indicates a purity of 99 percent (2N), where the sample contains a higher concentration of impurity atoms compared to samples with larger values of \( x \). This relationship is used to systematically categorize samples in a study investigating the influence of impurity content on grain boundary relaxation and thermal stability in nanocrystalline metals.
